texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polyolefin is one of the most widely applied polymer products for its lower cost and better processing performance. As the production of polyolefin increases, more and more environmental concerns are focused on the production procedure. The powder of polyolefin that generated from polymerization reactor usually adsorb certain amount of unreacted hydrocarbons. If these adsorbed substances cannot be desorbed effectively, they may cause critical smell and security problems during the storage and transportation procedure. Therefore, a nitrogen purging unit is employed to desorb the unreacted substances by reducing their partial pressure in commercialized polyolefin processes. The desorbed gas should be burnt with the purging nitrogen (Baker and Jacobs, 1996) subsequently. However, the tightening environmental regulations on CO2 emissions aimed at alleviating global warming are among the many driving forces behind the need
texts.text: Polyolefin is one of the most widely applied polymer products for its lower cost and better processing performance. As the production of polyolefin increases, more and more environmental concerns are focused on the production procedure. The powder of polyolefin that generated from polymerization reactor usually adsorb certain amount of unreacted hydrocarbons. If these adsorbed substances cannot be desorbed effectively, they may cause critical smell and security problems during the storage and transportation procedure. Therefore, a nitrogen purging unit is employed to desorb the unreacted substances by reducing their partial pressure in commercialized polyolefin processes. The desorbed gas should be burnt with the purging nitrogen (Baker and Jacobs, 1996) subsequently. However, the tightening environmental regulations on CO2 emissions aimed at alleviating global warming are among the many driving forces behind the need

texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The conventional methods for recovering effluent gas in polyethylene processes include compression condensing, membrane separation, cryogenic separation systems and pressure swing adsorption. Since the effluent gas content varies with the process, there is no single recovery technic can meet all effluent gas content conditions. Therefore, a systematic strategy is needed to guide the selection of recovery technologies. This article introduces a method to select the best recovery system for certain compositions of effluent gas. After searching the feasible content region for the recovery systems according to the specified concentration of recycle stream and outlet streams, it shows PSA system has the largest feasible region while compression condensation system has the smallest one. In comparison of economic and exergetic performance, the cryogenic separation system shows its outstanding peculiarity to recover the effluent gas. The obtained result is effective in guiding the design of recovery system.
texts.text: The conventional methods for recovering effluent gas in polyethylene processes include compression condensing, membrane separation, cryogenic separation systems and pressure swing adsorption. Since the effluent gas content varies with the process, there is no single recovery technic can meet all effluent gas content conditions. Therefore, a systematic strategy is needed to guide the selection of recovery technologies. This article introduces a method to select the best recovery system for certain compositions of effluent gas. After searching the feasible content region for the recovery systems according to the specified concentration of recycle stream and outlet streams, it shows PSA system has the largest feasible region while compression condensation system has the smallest one. In comparison of economic and exergetic performance, the cryogenic separation system shows its outstanding peculiarity to recover the effluent gas. The obtained result is effective in guiding the design of recovery system.

Nevertheless, the low pressure, low concentration and multicomponent nature of effluent gas makes it difficult to recover the individual components.
texts.text: for energy conservation and efficiency (Hasan et al., 2010; Gharaie et al., 2013). Therefore, the effluent gas should be recycled to realize cleaner production of polyolefin. Nevertheless, the low pressure, low concentration and multicomponent nature of effluent gas makes it difficult to recover the individual components.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us take a typical polyethylene (PE) process as an example. Its effluent components include ethylene, ethane, n-butene, isopentane, nitrogen and so on. Among these components, hydrocarbons could be separated by phase change operations such as compression condensing (Baker and Jacobs, 1996) and cryogenic separation (Valencia and Victory, 1990; Barnicki and Fair, 1992) because of their relatively higher boiling points. On the other hand, nitrogen whose boiling point is relatively lower could be separated by membrane (Ohlrogge et al., 1990) or pressure swing adsorption (Mehra and Stodghill, 1996; Mehra and Stodghill, 1997) (PSA) operations. In fact, either of membrane separation,
texts.text: Let us take a typical polyethylene (PE) process as an example. Its effluent components include ethylene, ethane, n-butene, isopentane, nitrogen and so on. Among these components, hydrocarbons could be separated by phase change operations such as compression condensing (Baker and Jacobs, 1996) and cryogenic separation (Valencia and Victory, 1990; Barnicki and Fair, 1992) because of their relatively higher boiling points. On the other hand, nitrogen whose boiling point is relatively lower could be separated by membrane (Ohlrogge et al., 1990) or pressure swing adsorption (Mehra and Stodghill, 1996; Mehra and Stodghill, 1997) (PSA) operations. In fact, either of membrane separation,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: pressure swing adsorption or cryogenic separation (Valencia and Victory, 1990) units can be employed to separate such a mixture. Baker and Jacobs (1996) proposed that integrating the membrane systems with typical compression-condensation unit can improve the purity and recovery of monomers in polyolefin resin degassing. Mehra and Stodghill (1997) invented an absorption process combining distillation unit for rejection of the reactor byproducts and recovery of monomers from the waste gas in polyolefin. Cryogenic separation is usually applied for air separation (Smith and Klosek, 2001), LNG process (Houshmand et al., 1997), separating carbon dioxide from hydrocarbons (Valencia and Victory, 1990; Smith and Klosek, 2001; Houshmand et al., 1997), while it is rarely utilized in effluent gas recovery process. These technologies are based on different separation principles which can result in different performance profiles. The selection of these technologies depends on the economic and efficiency aspects as well as the ease of future expansion. Although all of these technologies have been widely studied, the reported results are only instructive for given purification technologies. There lacks a systematical strategy to guide the purification technology selection for arbitrary effluent compositions inside the polyolefin plants, while this is the object of this article.
texts.text: pressure swing adsorption or cryogenic separation (Valencia and Victory, 1990) units can be employed to separate such a mixture. Baker and Jacobs (1996) proposed that integrating the membrane systems with typical compression-condensation unit can improve the purity and recovery of monomers in polyolefin resin degassing. Mehra and Stodghill (1997) invented an absorption process combining distillation unit for rejection of the reactor byproducts and recovery of monomers from the waste gas in polyolefin. Cryogenic separation is usually applied for air separation (Smith and Klosek, 2001), LNG process (Houshmand et al., 1997), separating carbon dioxide from hydrocarbons (Valencia and Victory, 1990; Smith and Klosek, 2001; Houshmand et al., 1997), while it is rarely utilized in effluent gas recovery process. These technologies are based on different separation principles which can result in different performance profiles. The selection of these technologies depends on the economic and efficiency aspects as well as the ease of future expansion. Although all of these technologies have been widely studied, the reported results are only instructive for given purification technologies. There lacks a systematical strategy to guide the purification technology selection for arbitrary effluent compositions inside the polyolefin plants, while this is the object of this article.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This article introduces a method to select the best recovery system for certain compositions of effluent gas. Four kinds of recovery systems are employed. They are compression condensation, membrane, cryogenic and PSA. Firstly, we search the feasible content region for the recovery systems according to the specified concentration of recycle stream and outlet streams. Then, we compare the annualized total revenue and exergetic efficiency of the recovery systems within the feasible region via optimization based method. All the process calculations are implemented in the Aspen Plus software, and the obtained result is effective in guiding the recovery system design.
texts.text: This article introduces a method to select the best recovery system for certain compositions of effluent gas. Four kinds of recovery systems are employed. They are compression condensation, membrane, cryogenic and PSA. Firstly, we search the feasible content region for the recovery systems according to the specified concentration of recycle stream and outlet streams. Then, we compare the annualized total revenue and exergetic efficiency of the recovery systems within the feasible region via optimization based method. All the process calculations are implemented in the Aspen Plus software, and the obtained result is effective in guiding the recovery system design.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 1 shows the outlet stream from the polymerization reactor which includes resin powder and un-desorbed reactant components. They are sent to a purge bin by nitrogen blowing. The resin powder stays in the purge bin for several minutes. During this time, the absorbed hydrocarbons are transferred from the resin powder to the gas phase, then discharged out of the purge bin. We assume the amount of absorbed hydrocarbons are fixed for the same reaction process and product grade. In addition, the residence time of resin powder in the purge bin is assumed enough long to desorb all the hydrocarbons. In other words, the absorbed gas can be removed completely. The compositions of typical effluent gas are shown in Table 1.
texts.text: Fig. 1 shows the outlet stream from the polymerization reactor which includes resin powder and un-desorbed reactant components. They are sent to a purge bin by nitrogen blowing. The resin powder stays in the purge bin for several minutes. During this time, the absorbed hydrocarbons are transferred from the resin powder to the gas phase, then discharged out of the purge bin. We assume the amount of absorbed hydrocarbons are fixed for the same reaction process and product grade. In addition, the residence time of resin powder in the purge bin is assumed enough long to desorb all the hydrocarbons. In other words, the absorbed gas can be removed completely. The compositions of typical effluent gas are shown in Table 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From Table 1, the components of effluent gas can be divided into three classes by dew points. They are C4+ hydrocarbons (hydrocarbons heavier than ethane), C2 hydrocarbons (ethylene and ethane) and nitrogen. C4+ hydrocarbons can be directly liquefied by compression condensing. Therefore, compression condensing is essential and widely used in the recovery system. On the other hand, nitrogen and C2 hydrocarbons can be separated by either membrane separation, pressure swing adsorption or cryogenic separation methods. Therefore, the selection of recovery system is important and it may change with the valuable components of effluent gas.
texts.text: From Table 1, the components of effluent gas can be divided into three classes by dew points. They are C4+ hydrocarbons (hydrocarbons heavier than ethane), C2 hydrocarbons (ethylene and ethane) and nitrogen. C4+ hydrocarbons can be directly liquefied by compression condensing. Therefore, compression condensing is essential and widely used in the recovery system. On the other hand, nitrogen and C2 hydrocarbons can be separated by either membrane separation, pressure swing adsorption or cryogenic separation methods. Therefore, the selection of recovery system is important and it may change with the valuable components of effluent gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among a variety of gas recovery systems, we propose four appropriate separation systems to recover the effluent gas from PE process. Each separation system has its application scope which should be found out. It means for the same recovery task, we can change the compositions of effluent gas to find the best separation system for recovering the effluent gas from a certain PE process. To calculate the application scope and operational performance of four separation systems, economic performance and exergetic efficiency is selected to analyze and discuss. From the results of analysis, the principles for guiding the recovery system design can be concluded.
texts.text: Among a variety of gas recovery systems, we propose four appropriate separation systems to recover the effluent gas from PE process. Each separation system has its application scope which should be found out. It means for the same recovery task, we can change the compositions of effluent gas to find the best separation system for recovering the effluent gas from a certain PE process. To calculate the application scope and operational performance of four separation systems, economic performance and exergetic efficiency is selected to analyze and discuss. From the results of analysis, the principles for guiding the recovery system design can be concluded.

texts.orig: The four known separation systems are presented in Figs. 2, 3, 4 and 6, where compression condensing is recognized as bench case.
texts.text: The four known separation systems are presented in Figs. 2, 3, 4 and 6, where compression condensing is recognized as bench case.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The bench case in Fig. 2 only employs compressioncondensing unit to recover hydrocarbons. In the figure, we can see the effluent gas is two-stage compressed from 0.1 MPaA to 0.45 MPaA and 2.0 MPaA respectively. After each compression, there are coolers and condensation tanks to liquefy C4+ hydrocarbons. The condensates are recycled to the reactor by pumps, while the uncondensed gas is sent to the flare.
texts.text: The bench case in Fig. 2 only employs compressioncondensing unit to recover hydrocarbons. In the figure, we can see the effluent gas is two-stage compressed from 0.1 MPaA to 0.45 MPaA and 2.0 MPaA respectively. After each compression, there are coolers and condensation tanks to liquefy C4+ hydrocarbons. The condensates are recycled to the reactor by pumps, while the uncondensed gas is sent to the flare.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After recovering C4+ hydrocarbon in the bench case, the remaining C2 hydrocarbons also need to be recovered from the tail gas. We propose three kinds of units to combine with the bench case. They are membrane separation, cryogenic separation and pressure swing adsorption. Let us introduce these combined systems.
texts.text: After recovering C4+ hydrocarbon in the bench case, the remaining C2 hydrocarbons also need to be recovered from the tail gas. We propose three kinds of units to combine with the bench case. They are membrane separation, cryogenic separation and pressure swing adsorption. Let us introduce these combined systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The principle of membrane separation is the adsorbing capacity and gas transfer rate differences between components. This permeation procedure is driven by pressure difference. We employ a kind of rubbery hydrocarbon membrane (Baker et al., 1998) that are specific in permeating hydrocarbons. Note that the separation performance of membrane is not only determined by its system parameters, it is also a function of the arrangement of membranes. An arrangement of three membranes in series structure is adopted in this article. We build up the mathematical model of this three membrane in Aspen Custom Modeler like Rodrigues et al. (2010) did and integrated it with Aspen Plus. In the mathematical model, the flux equation is based on solution-diffusion mechanism and it reads:

An arrangement of three membranes in series structure is adopted in this article. We build up the mathematical model of this three membrane in Aspen Custom Modeler like Rodrigues et al. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The series membrane structure is shown in Fig. 3. The compression-condensing unit is the same as the bench case. For ease of representation, part of this system is denoted by ellipsis in Figs. 3, 4 and 6. A part of the uncondensed gas from the bench case is used to transport the powder into the purge bin. We call it transport gas. The other part is sent into the membrane separation system. The low pressure hydrocarbon-enriched permeate streams of the first two membranes are sent back to the bench case for further separation, while the permeate stream of the third membrane is sent to flare to prevent accumulation of the non-condensable gases. The residual stream of the third membrane is nitrogen-enriched, it can be sent back to the purge bin.
texts.text: The series membrane structure is shown in Fig. 3. The compression-condensing unit is the same as the bench case. For ease of representation, part of this system is denoted by ellipsis in Figs. 3, 4 and 6. A part of the uncondensed gas from the bench case is used to transport the powder into the purge bin. We call it transport gas. The other part is sent into the membrane separation system. The low pressure hydrocarbon-enriched permeate streams of the first two membranes are sent back to the bench case for further separation, while the permeate stream of the third membrane is sent to flare to prevent accumulation of the non-condensable gases. The residual stream of the third membrane is nitrogen-enriched, it can be sent back to the purge bin.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The cryogenic separation is widely used in air separation (Smith and Klosek, 2001), LNG process (Houshmand et al., 1997), separating carbon dioxide from hydrocarbons (Valencia and Victory, 1990; Smith and Klosek, 2001; Houshmand et al., 1997), it is the first time proposed to be used in effluent gas recovery of polyolefin process. We propose a novel self-refrigeration system without any utilities. As shown in Fig. 4, the residual gas from compressioncondensing unit goes into the multi-stream heat exchanger (MHEX) as a hot stream. Being cooled in the MHEX, the hot stream is liquefied partially and separated in gas-liquid separator 1. The separated streams are the cold streams. To obtain enough approach temperatures between hot and cold streams, the turbines and valves should be used to produce both thermal (cold) exergy and work from pressure exergy. The gas stream is expanded in a turbine while the liquid stream is in a valve.
texts.text: The cryogenic separation is widely used in air separation (Smith and Klosek, 2001), LNG process (Houshmand et al., 1997), separating carbon dioxide from hydrocarbons (Valencia and Victory, 1990; Smith and Klosek, 2001; Houshmand et al., 1997), it is the first time proposed to be used in effluent gas recovery of polyolefin process. We propose a novel self-refrigeration system without any utilities. As shown in Fig. 4, the residual gas from compressioncondensing unit goes into the multi-stream heat exchanger (MHEX) as a hot stream. Being cooled in the MHEX, the hot stream is liquefied partially and separated in gas-liquid separator 1. The separated streams are the cold streams. To obtain enough approach temperatures between hot and cold streams, the turbines and valves should be used to produce both thermal (cold) exergy and work from pressure exergy. The gas stream is expanded in a turbine while the liquid stream is in a valve.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to the design specs of the MHEX, the exchanger minimum approach temperature (EMAT) cannot be lower than 2 ◦ C which can be determined by temperatureenthalpy profile for heat integration. According to the T -H curve shown in Fig. 5, the EMAT is 2.80 ◦ C, and any hot/cold utility is not required.
texts.text: According to the design specs of the MHEX, the exchanger minimum approach temperature (EMAT) cannot be lower than 2 ◦ C which can be determined by temperatureenthalpy profile for heat integration. According to the T -H curve shown in Fig. 5, the EMAT is 2.80 ◦ C, and any hot/cold utility is not required.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pressure swing adsorption (PSA) is a commonly used technology for the gas mixtures separation. As in all adsorption separation systems, the essential requirement is an adsorbent that preferentially adsorbs one component (or one family of related components) from a mixed feed. The fundamental of PSA has been described thoroughly by Ruthven et al. (1994). The hydrocarbon recovery is a function of system parameters such as feed purity, product purity, feed pressure, residue pressure and unit features such as the phase number of equalization, adsorbent, the number of adsorbers and so on.
texts.text: Pressure swing adsorption (PSA) is a commonly used technology for the gas mixtures separation. As in all adsorption separation systems, the essential requirement is an adsorbent that preferentially adsorbs one component (or one family of related components) from a mixed feed. The fundamental of PSA has been described thoroughly by Ruthven et al. (1994). The hydrocarbon recovery is a function of system parameters such as feed purity, product purity, feed pressure, residue pressure and unit features such as the phase number of equalization, adsorbent, the number of adsorbers and so on.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lacava et al. (1998) introduced some principles in PSA system design. Knaebel (1999) concluded that the product recovery of hydrogen for bulk separation during adsorption. In our recovery systems, the PSA can be regarded as a sequential process focusing on the initial and final states. Therefore, if we know the separation efficiency of effluent gas components in PSA according to the industrial data, Aspen Plus can simulate the process successfully.
texts.text: Lacava et al. (1998) introduced some principles in PSA system design. Knaebel (1999) concluded that the product recovery of hydrogen for bulk separation during adsorption. In our recovery systems, the PSA can be regarded as a sequential process focusing on the initial and final states. Therefore, if we know the separation efficiency of effluent gas components in PSA according to the industrial data, Aspen Plus can simulate the process successfully.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the optimal value. For a given recovery system and a given nitrogen content, one can run this problem to find a maximum value of C2 content. We specify six levels of nitrogen content, they are evenly distributed between 10% and 60%. Note that 10% and 60% are the lower bound and upper bound of industrial application. Recalling there are four recovery systems, we can totally obtain 24 values of C2 content. Combining these maximum C2 value with their corresponding nitrogen content, 24 points can be drawn in a rectangular coordinate whose x -coordinate represents X N2 and y -coordinate represents X C2 as shown in Fig. 7.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From Fig. 7, according to the variation tendency of 6 points in each recovery system, we use linear equations to fit these tendencies. The goodness of fit R 2 of each straight line is more than 0.99. The four linear functions are listed in Table 2. The limiting constraints of each recovery system are also presented in the table.
texts.text: From Fig. 7, according to the variation tendency of 6 points in each recovery system, we use linear equations to fit these tendencies. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The four straight lines of Table 2 are represented by green, blue, red and black lines in Fig. 7 respectively. They are the upper limit of X C2 of the four combined systems. For example, if the nitrogen and C2 component of certain effluent gas is located higher than the green line of Fig. 7, then it is not feasible to recover desired products by the bench case. From Fig. 7, we can also find that the feasible region of PSA system is the largest one, while that of the bench case is the smallest one. Note that, the region above the black line is the infeasible region for all the existing four recovery systems.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Comparison of the economic performance recovery systems
texts.text: 4.2. Comparison of the economic performance recovery systems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From Fig. 7 we can see that the feasible regions of the four recovery systems overlaps each other. As a consequence, there are several alternative recovery systems for a certain effluent gas component. The criterions should be chosen to determine the best recovery system. We compare economic performance of the alternative recovery systems in this section. The economic performance is measured by the annualized total revenue. Take the cryogenic separation system for instance, its annualized total revenue includes the profits of products and cost of operation:
texts.text: From Fig. 7 we can see that the feasible regions of the four recovery systems overlaps each other. As a consequence, there are several alternative recovery systems for a certain effluent gas component. The criterions should be chosen to determine the best recovery system. We compare economic performance of the alternative recovery systems in this section. The economic performance is measured by the annualized total revenue. Take the cryogenic separation system for instance, its annualized total revenue includes the profits of products and cost of operation:

texts.orig: Exergy Analysis (Kotas, 1995) is widely used in process synthesis, its advantage is the inherent capability of including all stream properties (temperature, pressure, and compositions).
texts.text: Exergy Analysis (Kotas, 1995) is widely used in process synthesis, its advantage is the inherent capability of including all stream properties (temperature, pressure, and compositions).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It focuses on the equipment units, rather than the flowsheet level. Because there is often a conflict between reducing exergy losses and reducing cost, exergy analysis is another important guideline to compare the performance and properties of these recovery systems.
texts.text: It focuses on the equipment units, rather than the flowsheet level. Because there is often a conflict between reducing exergy losses and reducing cost, exergy analysis is another important guideline to compare the performance and properties of these recovery systems.

texts.orig: The exergetic efficiency ( /DC1 E ) is defined as the useful exergy output to the exergy input:
texts.text: The exergetic efficiency ( /DC1 E ) is defined as the useful exergy output to the exergy input:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: /DC1 E = ∑ E -∑ E + = E out + E Wex E in + E Wco + E Qc + E Qh (8)

texts.label: text

It is the first point on the blue line which is a common line in the feasible region of three systems. The detailed calculating procedure is presented in the supporting information while the results are shown in Fig. 11.

texts.orig: Fig. 12 -The exergetic efficiency and annualized total revenue of three separation methods with 6 different X N2.
texts.text: Fig. 12 -The exergetic efficiency and annualized total revenue of three separation methods with 6 different X N2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: efficiency is. In the membrane separation, the pressure difference is the driving force for components separation. Permeate gas has a large exergy loss because pressure difference is large. Meanwhile the residual gas having a small exergy loss because the pressure difference is small. During the cryogenic separation, the pressure difference between inlet and outlet streams are large on account of expansion mechanism. Therefore, its exergy loss is larger than membrane separation. However, the temperature reduction of the cryogenic system assists the decrease of exergy loss. For the PSA process, the pressure drop in PSA bed is the most important factor influencing on exergy loss. Among these separation ways, the pressure drop in PSA bed is the largest. Consequently it has the largest exergy loss.

However, the temperature reduction of the cryogenic system assists the decrease of exergy loss. For the PSA process, the pressure drop in PSA bed is the most important factor influencing on exergy loss. Among these separation ways, the pressure drop in PSA bed is the largest. Consequently it has the largest exergy loss.

texts.orig: annualized total revenue is dropping rapidly. At the same time, the exergetic efficiency of cryogenic separation declines slightly as the increasing X N2 while annualized total revenue is keeping almost the same. On the other hand, the exergetic efficiency and annualized total revenue of PSA both drops as the increasing of X N2, meanwhile, no matter exergetic efficiency, or annualized total revenue is lower than other systems. We can see the compare results for exergetic efficiency are different from annualized total revenue, and there is no strong link between them. From this, the minimization of irreversibility achieved by Exergy Analysis can help to choose the best separation system.

From this, the minimization of irreversibility achieved by Exergy Analysis can help to choose the best separation system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recovering effluent gas from polyolefin plant is critical to its environmental discharge regulation and economic performance. The feasibility and economic performance of recovery systems is determined by the effluent component and the recover technology. This article compares four kinds of recovery systems in three aspects via optimization based approach. The obtained feasible region shows that PSA system has the largest feasible region while compression condensation system has the smallest one. After comparing economic performance of three combined systems, the cryogenic separation system is on the top of the three systems. In other words, the cryogenic separation system is the most economical one among three combined systems since it owns the largest area in the feasible region. In addition, the exergetic performance shows the membrane separation system has the highest exergetic efficiency, while that of the cryogenic separation system is very close to the membrane separation system. Since the compared membrane, cryogenic, PSA and compression condensation units are common in process industries, the proposed approach can be extended to other processes that have hydrocarbon containing gas effluent.
texts.text: Recovering effluent gas from polyolefin plant is critical to its environmental discharge regulation and economic performance. The feasibility and economic performance of recovery systems is determined by the effluent component and the recover technology. This article compares four kinds of recovery systems in three aspects via optimization based approach. The obtained feasible region shows that PSA system has the largest feasible region while compression condensation system has the smallest one. After comparing economic performance of three combined systems, the cryogenic separation system is on the top of the three systems. In other words, the cryogenic separation system is the most economical one among three combined systems since it owns the largest area in the feasible region. In addition, the exergetic performance shows the membrane separation system has the highest exergetic efficiency, while that of the cryogenic separation system is very close to the membrane separation system. Since the compared membrane, cryogenic, PSA and compression condensation units are common in process industries, the proposed approach can be extended to other processes that have hydrocarbon containing gas effluent.
